paper_map.title: Effect of alloying content on microstructure and mechanical properties of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels
paper_map.abstract_summary: A series of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels with varying C (0.6–1.5%), Mn (20–35%), and Al (6–12%) were fabricated. The microstructure, density, tensile properties, and impact toughness were examined to quantify alloying effects. Yield strength increases with Al and C but decreases with Mn, while impact toughness drops sharply for C > 1.2%, Mn < 25%, or Al > 10%. The results are attributed to κ-carbide precipitation and coarsening during solution treatment, and a density formula ρ = 8.1 – 0.102C – 0.10Al – 0.01Mn is proposed.
paper_map.research_objective: To systematically investigate the effects of C, Mn, and Al contents on the microstructure, density, tensile mechanical properties, and impact toughness of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels, and to establish quantitative relationships that guide alloy design.
material_systems: Nominal composition ranges: C 0.6–1.5%, Mn 20–35%, Al 6.0–12.0%
Key phases: austenite, ferrite, κ-carbides (intragranular and intergranular)
main_process_variables: Hot rolling to 16 mm diameter rods
Solution treatment: 1050–1100 °C for 2 h followed by water quenching
Tensile testing (gauge length 25 mm, diameter 5 mm, axis parallel to rod axis)
Density measurement by Archimedes drainage method
Alloy composition variation: C (0.6–1.5 wt%), Mn (20–35 wt%), Al (6–12 wt%)
expected_information_axis: microstructure (ferrite phase fraction, grain size, κ-carbide size and location)
tensile properties (yield strength, work hardening rate)
key_sections: 1. Introduction: background on FeMnAlC steels, alloying effects on density and mechanical properties, role of κ-carbides, research gaps and objectives
2. Experimental: melting, casting, hot rolling, solution treatment, tensile and Charpy impact testing, density measurement, chemical compositions in Table 1
3. Results: 3.1 Mechanical properties and fracture behavior; 3.2 Microstructural characterization by EBSD and TEM

4. Discussion: 4.1 Quantitative alloying effects on microstructure and density; 4.2 Precipitation effects on mechanical properties and impact toughness; 4.3 Alloying effects on work hardening rate and underlying mechanisms
